speaker: interviewer
text: What would you call the student? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other kinds of questions that you-- Mr. Damski, can you introduce these two lovely ladies to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is my wife, Christine; and on the left, my daughter, Eva Muchnick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mrs. Damski, is there something that you would like to say about your life with-- with your husband, Mr. Damski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after so many years, what can you say? [LAUGHTER] I must be happy. We'll be together since '41-- 1941. So it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: A long time, but it went by fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And-- and Eva, what was it like to grow up with two survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- it was a maturing experience very early to know-- to hear the stories from as far back as I can remember. So it put a-- a different slant on one's life-- a sobering and maturing experience. And I'm very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And how was it with two different backgrounds? Right? Did you find--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they don't practice. They're not practicing, so it didn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a problem, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you yourself right now are a practicing Jew, is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Judaism, yeah. We're raising our children Jewish. And they're-- they're-- they enjoy celebrating all the holidays. Yeah. They caused a little uproar in our community, because one of-- [LAUGHS] one of our-- my daughters was photographed sitting on Santa's knee. And it was the front page of the local paper. And some of our friends who are Jewish all commented on that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, it's-- they still believe in the tooth fairy, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if you could say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. If things like that bothers them-- if they would go to a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait, but you don't-- you don't know that. You didn't talk--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is-- is there anything else that-- that anyone would like to add at this point? Before we--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll-- you know what? It-- it's hard, especially here in Los Angeles. Neither the Jews or the Poles are tolerant here in this town.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had very bitter experiences. I told Raul that after Johnny got his medal for helping the Jews, he lost his--

speaker: interviewee
text: --acquaintances. And when I went to the big dinner that we had for the rescuers, some people who came and paid $500 a plate ask-- told me, after what you went through, why did you marry a Pole? So things like this are very unpleasant. And you know, when we lived in Detroit, we had friends, real friends. And they knew about me, I-- I think. And here is different. I don't know what makes it so different here. That-- yeah. I-- I myself, I'm talking about me, I feel almost like a outcast, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you-- you married a non-Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not because I married, but because of the intolerance of-- of both sides. There's nothing wrong between us except the attitude of other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you find the same thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] I got used to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I look at-- everybody has his opinion.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have to share their opinion. I don't have to associate with them. I have my belief, and I will never-- I'm never shy of expressing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that-- that creates sometimes some enemies. But that's fine with me. You have to strike it. I have to tell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let them think. That's the whole point. Let them think. And when they start to think, that the first step to-- to tolerance. Let's put it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the two of you together proved something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we never had any-- any-- when it comes to our different background, we had never an argument-- never. We might criticize the Poles-- both. And we probably criticize once in a while the Jews too.

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- for different reason-- not for-- for them being Jews, but perhaps there's a Jew that cheated me out of money. Then we talk about it-- but only as a cheater, not as a Jew. So we never had any problem. I-- I-- my mother now was a religious, I could say, person-- not very, but religious. And I had never-- never any problem with her in an argument. Because we-- we both see what's right, and we both see what's wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are tolerant, good people.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's only when people think that they are right and you are wrong, then it's a problem. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you find that you're a more tolerant person than most people, because of your upbringing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think I understand better. And I sometimes-- when I hear the criticisms of my friends on-- on either side, I can point things out to them-- you know, how I had a Catholic upbringing, so I understand. And I know people, even my generation, who are Jewish and who think of a Christmas tree or Santa Claus as a religious symbol. And I say, well to Christians, that's not a religious symbol. It's a secular symbol. And they don't understand that. And I say, believe me, it is. That's a secular symbol. So I find that I can understand both sides. And I think that's an advantage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And our granddaughters love the Christmas tree and the presents. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I hope that my granddaughters will grow up not to be-- I don't expect them to be prejudice.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't expect them to be prejudiced. I-- I know that they-- they love me, and I love them. And I think perhaps this will, when they grow up, and they think, they will think of their father as a non-Jew-- then--

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll give them a different perception.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's fine. See, I-- I-- I'm-- I'm glad. I'm actually glad that they go and learn Hebrew. I would know-- like to know Hebrew myself. I'm a little bit too old for that. But I'm glad that they do it. And I want them to-- to know other languages and other cultures so they-- they-- they grow up tolerating. Because if they not going to be tolerant when they children, when they grow up, it's too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: Their-- their two closest friends at school, actually, two of their closest friends for both of them are both Muslim.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why they're-- yeah, it's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and what their friends say, they're-- they bring their friends to the temple all the time-- Purim or Hanukkah. And their friends love the Jewish holidays. So I think it's very--

speaker: interviewee
text: Speaking of Muslims, my friend, Harvey Soner, the-- the one that sponsored so many people to go to Jerusalem, he went to Albania and sponsored a group of Muslims that helped Jews during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's really the way everybody is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it may be something positive will come out of-- a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: From-- from-- from-- yes-- from all of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: That would take another 4,000 years maybe. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much, all of you--

speaker: interviewer
text: --for taking part in this. Really, it was a real honor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And say thank you to-- to Spielberg for doing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Spielberg should be-- should be-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Proud, very proud of what's he is doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure he's proud of what he's doing. And I think he's doing a tremendous job-- tremendous job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you're-- you're a big part of this-- a big part.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we thank you very much.
